Failing doesn't count as a lesson if you're not developing a new skill from each one.

I had direction. I had a goal. I went straight at it for years, but none of my businesses worked until I had built up my skill set.

Each failed opportunity developed a different skill that I

Ecom products to avoid at all costs:
($1B+ in sales across my brands taught me to walk away from every one of these)

- Under 70% gross margin. Gross margin compression has killed more of my brands than bad new product launches ever did.

- Nobody can see it being used in public

tweets.text:
Hot take: generic group messages kill ownership.

U drop something in a 50-person Slack, shoot an "@ channel", and hope someone picks it up?

Good luck on ever getting it done.

It's not micromanagement to call people out directly.

It's the bare minimum if u want things to move.

I almost didn't move to New York.

Before Bustem, didn't have a team. Had a decent set up where I was. Low overhead.

It was easy. Comfortable. Every practical reason said: STAY.

Moved anyway.

Within 6 weeks I had dinners with founders doing $30M+/yr, closed 2 deals from

If u don't ask, u don't get.

Normies hint around what they want.
Scared of getting a "no"

Meanwhile killers:
> Ask for raises, promotions, credits, intros, favors
> Push for the green light: "Can we start today?"
> Quote a price upfront. Then ask for 20% more.
